L1.area: Unique identifier generation and character set encoding logic

L2.description: A cryptographic weakness exists where a random identifier generation routine uses a bitmask derived from the character set length to map random bytes to characters. When the character set size is not a power of two, the bitmask silently discards valid characters, dramatically reducing the effective entropy of generated identifiers. This makes the identifiers predictable and vulnerable to brute-force attacks when used in security-sensitive contexts such as session tokens.

L3.area: Base-58 and base-62 encoding routines in the unique ID generation library
L3.description: A cryptographic weakness exists in the base-58 and base-62 random identifier generation routines where a bitmask derived directly from the character set length (rather than the next power of two) is used to map random bytes to character indices. Because the character set sizes are not powers of two, the bitwise AND operation silently zeroes certain bits, reducing the base-62 output to only 32 distinct characters and the base-58 output to only 16. This dramatically reduces the entropy of generated identifiers, making them predictable and vulnerable to brute-force attacks.

ground_truth.reason: The gen macro computes MASK as alphabet_length - 1 instead of next_power_of_two(alphabet_length) - 1, causing the bitwise AND to discard valid character indices when the alphabet size is not a power of two, reducing base62 output to 32 characters and base58 to 16.
